title: Modeling Parts Framework & Parametric Concepts Knowledge Check

questions.question: What is the correct order of the four phases in the Creo design process?
questions.rationale: The Creo design process follows four phases in order: Model (create 3D parts), Assemble (combine parts), Document (generate 2D drawings), and Analyze (validate designs). This sequence reflects the natural progression from individual part creation to final design validation.

questions.question: In the model creation process, what should you do BEFORE creating features in Creo?
options: Verify the model against design intent
Select the extrude tool and start sketching immediately
Plan the feature sequence, base feature, and sketch references
Save the file to the working directory
questions.rationale: The model creation process follows a Plan-Create-Verify cycle. Planning involves determining the base feature, feature creation order, sketch planes, and parent-child relationships before you start modeling. Experienced designers spend 30-40% of their time in the planning phase.

questions.question: Which parametric modeling concept ensures that a change to a part model automatically updates all assemblies and drawings referencing it?
questions.rationale: Design associativity is the automatic link between related Creo files (parts, assemblies, drawings). When a part dimension changes, every assembly containing that part and every drawing referencing it updates automatically, eliminating manual synchronization.

questions.question: What is the correct file extension for a Creo assembly file?
questions.rationale: Creo uses .asm for assembly files, .prt for part files, and .drw for drawing files. Assembly files contain a collection of parts and sub-assemblies positioned using placement constraints. The .cad extension is not used by Creo.
